answer: The mean action value (Q) accounts for in-flight parallel descents by incorporating virtual loss into its calculation, where effective_visits = visits + virtual_loss and effective_value = total_value - virtual_loss, so Q = effective_value / effective_visits, which discourages multiple threads from exploring the same edge by making it appear less valuable when virtual loss is high. [S6]

The MCTS player can reuse part of the previous search tree when the opponent has made exactly one move (delta-1 case) by descending through the child matching the opponent's last action and using it as the new root, provided that child is not terminal and has at least one child. A candidate subtree is disqualified if the matched child is a leaf (visited but unexpanded) or terminal, as promoting it would leave the Select phase with no children to choose from, causing a fall-through to a fresh tree build. [S3] [S4]

When the same MCTS player is queried again after the opponent has made exactly one move (delta-1 case), it descends through `game.LastAction()` to find the matching child in `saved_root_` and uses that child as the new root if it is a non-terminal node with at least one child; otherwise, it falls through to a fresh build. A candidate subtree is disqualified if the matched child is a leaf (visited but unexpanded) or terminal, because promoting it to root would leave `Select` with no children to choose from. [S3]

The MCTS player can reuse part of the previous search tree when the opponent has made exactly one move (delta-1 case) by descending through `game.LastAction()` to find a matching child in the saved subtree and using it as the new root, provided the child is not terminal and has at least one child. A candidate subtree is disqualified if the matched child is a visited-but-unexpanded leaf (no children), as promoting it to root would leave the `Select` step with no actions to choose, or if the child is terminal, as terminal positions are handled separately by the outer search loop. [S3][S11][S12]

When the same MCTS player is queried again after the opponent has made exactly one move (delta-1), it attempts to reuse part of the previous search tree by descending through `game.LastAction()` to find a matching child in `saved_root_` and using that child as the new root, provided it is not terminal and has children. [S3] A candidate subtree is disqualified if the matched child is a visited-but-unexpanded leaf (no children), as promoting it to root would leave `Select` with nothing to choose, causing a fall-through to the fresh-build path. [S1] Terminal positions are also excluded from reuse and handled separately by the outer search loop. [S1] If no valid match is found, the arena is dropped and a fresh tree is built. [S3]

The mean action value accounts for in-flight parallel descents by treating each virtual loss as a -1 outcome from the parent's perspective, which reduces the effective value and increases the effective visit count in the Q calculation, thereby discouraging multiple threads from exploring the same edge simultaneously. [S2] The formula used is effective_visits = visits + virtual_loss, effective_value = total_value - virtual_loss, and Q = effective_value / effective_visits, as demonstrated in the test where a child with VL=1 and no real visits yields Q = -1, and with two real wins and one VL yields Q = (2 - 1) / 3. [S1] This mechanism is implemented via AddVirtualLoss and RemoveVirtualLoss methods that atomically adjust the virtual_loss counter. [S3] Sequential search leaves virtual_loss at 0, but concurrent drivers use it to penalize in-flight visits. [S2]

The mean action value accounts for in-flight parallel descents by treating each virtual loss as a -1 outcome from the parent's perspective, which reduces the effective value and increases the effective visit count in the Q calculation, thereby discouraging multiple threads from exploring the same edge simultaneously. [S2] The formula used is effective_visits = visits + virtual_loss, effective_value = total_value - virtual_loss, and Q = effective_value / effective_visits, as demonstrated in the test where a child with VL=1 and no real visits yields Q = -1, and with two real wins (Q=+1) and one VL yields Q = (2 - 1) / 3. [S1] This mechanism is implemented via AddVirtualLoss and RemoveVirtualLoss methods that atomically adjust the virtual_loss_ counter. [S3] Sequential search leaves virtual_loss = 0 throughout, but concurrent drivers use this mechanism to penalize in-flight visits. [S2]

The mean action value accounts for in-flight parallel descents by treating each virtual loss as a -1 outcome from the parent's perspective, which reduces the effective value and increases the effective visit count in the Q calculation, thereby discouraging multiple threads from exploring the same edge simultaneously. [S2] The formula used is effective_visits = visits + virtual_loss, effective_value = total_value - virtual_loss, and Q = effective_value / effective_visits, as shown in the test where a child with VL=1 and no real visits yields Q = -1, and with two real wins (Q=+1) and one VL yields Q = (2 - 1) / 3. [S1] This mechanism is implemented via AddVirtualLoss and RemoveVirtualLoss methods that atomically adjust the virtual_loss_ counter, ensuring concurrent searches correctly reflect in-flight visits. [S3] Sequential search leaves virtual_loss at 0, but concurrent drivers use it to penalize overlapping descents. [S2]

The batched inference server decides between a single-buffered synchronous path and a two-deep pipelined path based on whether the model implements `IAsyncForwardModel`; if it does (as with `CudaTransformerFp16`/`CudaTransformerFp8`), the executor uses two pinned slots and pipelines submit/wait two-deep, otherwise it uses a single pinned slot and the original sync path [S3][S4][S8]. The executor publishes a coherent set of mirrored weights to in-flight forward passes by flipping the `mirror_version_` seqlock-style counter only after `Mirror()` completes, ensuring readers see a consistent set of weights [S1].

The batched inference server decides to use a two-deep pipelined path when the model implements `IAsyncForwardModel` (e.g., FP16/FP8 transformer replicas), otherwise it uses a single-buffered synchronous path for sync-only models (CudaMlp, SplitCudaMlp, FP32 CudaTransformer, SplitCudaTransformer) [S4]. The executor publishes a coherent set of mirrored weights to in-flight forward passes by draining in-flight async slots before mirroring and flipping a seqlock-style version counter only after mirroring completes, ensuring readers see a fully consistent parameter set [S8][S9].

The batched inference server decides between a single-buffered synchronous path and a two-deep pipelined path based on whether the model implements `IAsyncForwardModel`; if it does (e.g., `CudaTransformerFp16` / `CudaTransformerFp8`), the executor uses two pinned slots and pipelines submit/wait two-deep, otherwise it uses a single pinned slot and the original sync path [S1]. The `Mirror()` method publishes a coherent set of mirrored weights to in-flight forward passes by copying parameters from the trainer's write-side model into the server's read-side model between batches, and only flipping the seqlock-style version counter (`mirror_version_`) after `Mirror()` completes, ensuring any in-flight `ForwardBatch` sees a fully-coherent set of parameters [S1].

The batched inference server decides between a single-buffered synchronous path and a two-deep pipelined path based on whether the model implements `IAsyncForwardModel`; if it does (e.g., `CudaTransformerFp16` / `CudaTransformerFp8`), the executor uses two pinned slots and pipelines submit/wait two-deep, otherwise it uses a single pinned slot and the original sync path [S2]. The weight mirror publishes a coherent set of mirrored weights to in-flight forward passes by using a seqlock-style version counter (`mirror_version_`) that is only flipped after `Mirror()` completes, ensuring any in-flight `ForwardBatch` sees a fully-coherent set of parameters [S3].

The global-L2-norm gradient clipping is arranged by accumulating the squared-gradient sum across all parameter buffers using atomicAdd<double> in sum_sq_kernel, which is launched for each gradient buffer (weights and biases) with a shared device memory accumulator that is zeroed before the kernels run and copied to the host after all kernels complete [S2][S3]. The L2 weight-decay term is never affected by the clip ratio because the grad_scale argument multiplies only the data gradient (grads[i]) before the weight-decay term (weight_decay * params[i]) is added in the Adam and SGD update kernels [S8][S9][S10][S11][S12][S13]. This ensures that weight decay is applied to the unscaled parameters, preserving the intended regularization effect independent of gradient clipping [S5].

The FP32 fused-attention tensor-core (WMMA) forward kernel is used for head dimensions kDh ∈ {16, 32} [S1]. The larger head dimension kDh=64 is excluded because its shared memory footprint (~96 KB) exceeds the per-CTA opt-in cap, so it falls back to the scalar one-thread-per-row kernel [S1].

The FP32 fused-attention implementation uses the tensor-core (WMMA) forward kernel for head dimensions `d_h ∈ {16, 32}` and falls back to the scalar kernel for `d_h ∈ {4, 8, 64}`. The `d_h=64` case is excluded because its shared memory footprint (~96 KB) exceeds the per-CTA static cap of 48 KB, making it unsuitable for the WMMA path without dynamic shared memory opt-in, which is not used in the FP32 trainer. [S8][S9]
The cache variable is `AZ_GAME_XQ_GIT_TAG` and the pinned tag is `v0.2.7`; the unit-test build is gated by the `AZ_BUILD_TESTS` option. [S5] [S4]

The cache variable is AZ_GAME_XQ_GIT_TAG and the pinned tag is v0.2.7, which select the concrete game compiled into the engine binaries; the unit-test build is gated by the AZ_BUILD_TESTS option, which defaults to OFF and enables tests when set to ON. [S5][S4]

The global-L2-norm gradient clipping is arranged by first accumulating the squared-gradient sum across all parameter buffers using the `sum_sq_kernel` with `atomicAdd<double>` into a single device memory location, then copying the result to the host to compute the total norm and derive the clip ratio `grad_scale` [S3][S4][S7][S11]. The squared-gradient sum is accumulated by launching `sum_sq_kernel` for each parameter gradient buffer (e.g., weights and biases of each layer) and atomically adding their contributions to a shared `d_sum_sq` buffer [S3][S4]. The L2 weight-decay term is never affected by the clip ratio because the `grad_scale` multiplies only the data gradient before the weight-decay term is added in the optimizer kernels (e.g., `adam_kernel` computes `g = grad_scale * grads[i] + weight_decay * params[i]`) [S8][S10][S11][S12][S13][S14]. This ensures weight decay is applied to the unscaled parameters, preserving its intended effect independent of gradient clipping.

The cache variable is `AZ_GAME_XQ_GIT_TAG` and the pinned tag is `"v0.2.7"`; the unit-test build is gated by the `AZ_BUILD_TESTS` option, which defaults to OFF and enables testing and adds the `tests` subdirectory when ON. [S4][S6]

The cache variable is `AZ_GAME_XQ_GIT_TAG` and the pinned tag is `v0.2.7`, which selects the concrete game compiled into the engine binaries. The unit-test build is gated by the `AZ_BUILD_TESTS` option, which is OFF by default and must be enabled to build and link the tests. [S5][S4]

CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F_FAST_TF32 makes the FP32 trainer GEMMs run on tensor cores [S2][S4][S10]. The FP8 entry point that produces FP32 output does not fuse bias in the epilogue because cuBLASLt requires bias dtype to match output dtype, and the replica only mirrors FP16 biases [S1][S3][S9].

The tensor-core (WMMA) forward kernel is used for head dimensions kDh ∈ {16, 32} in the FP32 fused-attention implementation, as determined by the FlashAttnFp32UseWmma() constexpr function which returns true only for these values [S1]. The larger head dimension kDh=64 is excluded because its shared memory footprint (~96 KB) exceeds the per-CTA opt-in cap, making it fall back to the scalar one-thread-per-row kernel [S1]. This is further confirmed by the LaunchFlashAttnForwardImpl_ function, which conditionally invokes the WMMA kernel only when FlashAttnFp32UseWmma<kDh>() is true [S3]. The performance optimization tracker also states that the FP32 trainer uses TF32 WMMA forward at d_h ∈ {16, 32} while backward stays scalar [S5]. The test utility UsesWmma explicitly returns true only for d_h == 16 || d_h == 32 [S4]. The exclusion of kDh=64 is due to shared memory constraints, as noted in the comment accompanying FlashAttnFp32UseWmma() [S1]. No other head dimensions use the WMMA forward kernel in the FP32 trainer. Thus, the WMMA forward kernel is used for kDh ∈ {16, 32}, and kDh=64 is excluded due to excessive shared memory usage. [S1][S3][S5][S4]

The squared-gradient sum is accumulated across all parameter buffers by launching the `sum_sq_kernel` for each gradient buffer (weights and biases) with a shared device memory pointer `d_sum_sq`, which is zeroed before the first kernel and updated via `atomicAdd<double>` in each kernel invocation [S2][S3]. The global-L2-norm gradient clipping is arranged so that the L2 weight-decay term is never affected by the clip ratio because the `grad_scale` (clip ratio) multiplies only the data gradient in the optimizer kernels (e.g., `adam_kernel` and `sgd_momentum_kernel`), while the weight-decay term is added independently as `weight_decay * params[i]` [S8][S9][S10][S5]. This ensures the weight-decay term is not scaled by the clip ratio. [S5] explicitly states: "The Adam / SGD kernels take a `grad_scale` argument so the clip ratio multiplies the data gradient (but not the weight-decay term) inside the update." [S8] shows the computation: `const float g = grad_scale * grads[i] + weight_decay * params[i];`, confirming the weight-decay term is outside the `grad_scale` multiplication. [S2][S3] show the norm computation and `grad_scale` derivation, which is then passed to the optimizer kernels. [S1] and [S4] detail the `sum_sq_kernel` implementation using shared memory reduction and `atomicAdd<double>` for accumulating squared gradients. [S7] confirms the same pattern in the transformer backend via `ComputeGradientL2Norm_()`. No other mechanism for gradient clipping or weight-decay interaction is described in the sources. Thus, the design isolates the clip ratio to the data gradient only. [S2][S3][S5][S8][S9][S10]

The compute type that makes FP32 trainer GEMMs run on tensor cores is `CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F_FAST_TF32` [S2][S4][S10]. The FP8 entry point that produces FP32 output (`RunFp8To32`) does not fuse bias in the epilogue because cuBLASLt requires the bias dtype to match the output dtype (FP32), but the replica only mirrors FP16 biases, so the caller adds the FP16 bias via a separate kernel [S1][S3][S9][S20].

The global-L2-norm gradient clipping is arranged so that the L2 weight-decay term is never affected by the clip ratio by scaling only the data gradient with `grad_scale` before adding the weight-decay term in the Adam and SGD update kernels, as seen in `adam_kernel` where `g = grad_scale * grads[i] + weight_decay * params[i]` [S12]. The squared-gradient sum is accumulated across all parameter buffers by launching `sum_sq_kernel` on each parameter gradient buffer, using a single device-side double `d_grad_sum_sq_` that is zeroed before each `ApplyOptimizer_` call and updated via `atomicAdd<double>` across all blobs from `EnumerateParams_()` [S9][S14]. The `sum_sq_kernel` computes `g[i]^2` per thread and reduces via shared memory and atomics to accumulate the total squared gradient norm [S14]. This accumulated sum is then copied to the host to compute the total norm, which determines `grad_scale` for clipping [S8][S9]. The `grad_scale` is passed to the optimizer kernels, ensuring only the gradient term is scaled, leaving weight decay unclipped [S12][S10]. The mechanism is consistent across MLP and transformer backends, with the transformer using `ComputeGradientL2Norm_()` which internally uses the same `sum_sq_kernel` reduction over all parameter blobs [S8][S9]. The `sum_sq_kernel` is explicitly designed for this global reduction, with the caller responsible for zeroing the output before launch [S14]. Thus, gradient clipping scales only the gradient contribution, preserving the integrity of the weight-decay term, and the squared gradients are summed globally via atomic additions across all buffers [S9][S12][S14].

The compute type that makes the FP32 trainer GEMMs run on tensor cores is `CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F_FAST_TF32` [S8]. The FP8 entry point that produces FP32 output does not fuse the bias in the epilogue because cuBLASLt requires the bias dtype to match the output dtype, and the replica biases are kept as FP16, so the caller adds the bias separately [S12][S14].

CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F_FAST_TF32 makes the FP32 trainer GEMMs run on tensor cores [S3][S6]. The FP8 entry point that produces FP32 output does not fuse bias because cuBLASLt requires bias dtype to match output dtype and the replica only mirrors FP16 biases [S2][S10][S13].

The measured slowdown was ~3.7× relative to the old board-only shape [S1]. FlashAttention recovered most of the loss, providing a 2.14× speedup on the new shape [S2].

The measured slowdown was ~3.7× relative to the old board-only shape [S1]. The optimization that recovered most of the loss was FlashAttention, which improved throughput by 2.14× on the new shape [S2].

The transformer is split between layers: the first n_layers/2 layers (including embedding) reside on device A, and the remaining layers plus final norm and head on device B. [S1] The boundary traffic per forward pass is one [B, T, d_model] BF16 tensor in each direction. [S1] At chess scale (B=256, T=64, d_model=512), this amounts to 16 MB per direction. [S1]

The transformer is split between layers: the first n_layers/2 layers live on device A and the rest on device B. [S3]

The transformer is split between layers: the first n_layers/2 layers (including embedding) reside on device A, and the remaining layers plus final norm and head on device B. [S1] The seam involves one [B, T, d_model] BF16 tensor copied in each direction per forward pass via cudaMemcpyPeer. [S1] At chess scale (B=256, T=64, d_model=512), this amounts to 16 MB per direction. [S1] This corresponds to roughly 0.5 ms over a PCIe Gen5 x16 link. [S1] For chess-scale (T=64), one boundary crossing is considered fine. [S1]

The daemon decides which model loader to call based on the checkpoint's schema version: v3 triggers `CudaMlp::Load`, v5 triggers `CudaTransformer::Load`, and any other version results in a 404 error [S2][S5]. The number of loaded checkpoints kept resident on the GPU is bounded by the `--model-cache-size` flag, which defaults to 2 and uses LRU eviction [S1][S4][S7].

The daemon decides which model loader to call based on the checkpoint's schema version: v3 triggers `CudaMlp::Load`, v5 triggers `CudaTransformer::Load`, and any other version returns a 404 [S7]. The number of loaded checkpoints kept resident on the GPU is bounded by the `--model-cache-size` flag, which defaults to 2 and enforces LRU eviction [S2].

The daemon decides which model loader to call based on the checkpoint's schema version: version 3 triggers `CudaMlp::Load`, version 5 triggers `CudaTransformer::Load`, and any other version results in a 404 response [S2][S5]. The number of loaded checkpoints kept resident on the GPU is bounded by the `--model-cache-size` flag, which defaults to 2 and uses LRU eviction [S1][S3][S4].

The README flags that entrypoints must adjust model input/output widths (`kInputSize`, `kOutputSize`) to match the new game's serializer output and handle history by wiring a real `RingBufferView` if `kHistoryLookback > 0` [S1]. The template layer under `include/az/` and `src/az/` is supposed to stay untouched [S1].

The README flags that entrypoints must adjust model input/output widths (`kInputSize`, `kOutputSize`) to match the new game's serializer output and handle history by wiring up a real `RingBufferView` if `kHistoryLookback > 0` [S1]. The template layer under `include/az/` and `src/az/` is supposed to stay untouched [S1].

The tensor-core (WMMA) forward kernel is used for head dimensions 16 and 32 in the FP32 fused-attention implementation, as determined by the `FlashAttnFp32UseWmma` function which returns true only for `kDh == 16 || kDh == 32` [S1]. The larger head dimension of 64 is excluded because its shared memory footprint (~96 KB) exceeds the per-CTA opt-in cap, making it fall back to the scalar one-thread-per-row kernel [S1]. This is further confirmed by the FP32 trainer using TF32 WMMA forward only at `d_h ∈ {16, 32}` while backward stays scalar [S5]. The WMMA path requires `kDh % 8 == 0` for TF32 m16n16k8, but even though 64 satisfies this, it is excluded due to shared memory constraints [S1]. The scalar fallback is used for `kDh ∈ {4, 8, 64}` [S1]. The test utility `UsesWmma` also confirms that only `d_h == 16 || d_h == 32` uses the WMMA path [S4]. The FP16 inference replica uses WMMA for `d_h ∈ {16, 32, 64}` but this is separate from the FP32 trainer's WMMA usage [S5][S7]. The FP32 WMMA forward kernel launch is guarded by `if constexpr (FlashAttnFp32UseWmma<kDh>())` [S3]. The shared memory footprint for `kDh=64` is explicitly stated to exceed the per-CTA opt-in cap (~96 KB) [S1]. The `d_h=64` case in FP32 falls back to scalar due to SMEM constraints, unlike in FP16 where it uses dynamic shared memory [S1][S5][S7]. The FP32 trainer's WMMA forward is limited to `d_h ∈ {16, 32}` for these reasons [S1][S5]. The exclusion of 64 is not due to precision or WMMA applicability but solely due to shared memory limits [S1]. The scalar kernels are used for unsupported shapes including 64 [S7].

The daemon decides which model loader to call based on the checkpoint's schema version: v3 triggers `CudaMlp::Load`, v5 triggers `CudaTransformer::Load`, and any other version results in a 404 response [S2][S6]. The number of loaded checkpoints kept resident on the GPU is bounded by the `--model-cache-size` flag, which defaults to 2 and enforces LRU eviction [S1][S4][S5].

The README flags entrypoint-level concerns about model input/output widths and history handling when retargeting the engine to a new game. [S8] The template layer that is supposed to stay untouched is everything under `include/az/**` and `src/az/**`, as these are templated on the game API and remain unchanged. [S1]

The README flags that entrypoints must adjust model input/output widths (`kInputSize`, `kOutputSize`) to match the new game's serializer output and handle history by wiring a real `RingBufferView` if `kHistoryLookback > 0` [S1]. The template layer under `include/az/` and `src/az/` is supposed to stay untouched [S1].

The checkpoint writer currently emits the V5 schema, which appends two float material targets (`material_own`, `material_opp`) per row over the previous V4 layout. [S2]  
The markdown describing the format is out of date at `src/az/train/replay-checkpoint.md`, which states the writer always emits the compact V4 row layout. [S3]

The GUI server reconstructs the per-artifact directory layout by reading the `[storage]` table from `run.toml` in the resolved config directory and resolving each path against `storage.root_dir` via `readStorageLayout`, mirroring the C++ trainer's resolution so both use the same layout [S6][S7][S8]. The environment variables `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` (explicit pin) and `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` (auto-pick newest `run.toml` one level deep) decide whether the GUI pins to one run or auto-picks the newest one [S1][S2][S3][S4][S5][S6].

The GUI server reconstructs the per-artifact directory layout by reading the `[storage]` table from `run.toml` in the resolved config directory and resolving each path against `storage.root_dir` via `readStorageLayout`, mirroring the C++ trainer's resolution so both use the same layout [S3][S6][S7][S8]. The environment variables `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` (explicit pin) and `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` (auto-pick newest `run.toml` one level deep) decide whether the GUI pins to one run or auto-picks the newest one [S1][S2][S3][S4][S5][S6].

The V4 layout, which V5 builds upon, appends `opp_legal_indices[k_dim]`, `opp_target_values[k_dim]`, and `uint8_t opp_present` after the V2 ply field for every row. [S1]  
The markdown that describes this format out of date is `src/az/train/replay-checkpoint.md`, which states the writer always emits the compact V4 row layout, while the writer actually emits V5. [S3]

The GUI server reconstructs the per-artifact directory layout by reading `run.toml`'s `[storage]` table from the resolved config directory and joining each path against `storage.root_dir` via `readStorageLayout`, matching the C++ trainer's resolution. [S1][S2][S3][S6][S7][S8][S9][S12][S13][S14][S15][S17][S18][S19] The environment variables `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` (explicit pin) and `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` (auto-pick newest `run.toml` one level deep) decide whether the GUI pins to one run or auto-picks the newest. [S1][S2][S3][S4][S6][S7][S12][S15]

The GUI server reconstructs the per-artifact directory layout by reading the `[storage]` table from `run.toml` in the active config directory and joining relative paths onto `storage.root_dir` to resolve artifact directories like `model_checkpoint_dir` and `gui_game_trace_dir` [S1][S2][S3][S5][S6][S7]. The environment variables `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` and `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` decide whether the GUI pins to one run or auto-picks the newest one: `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` explicitly pins the GUI to a config directory, while `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` is used as the enumeration root to auto-pick the newest `run.toml` one level deep when `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` is unset [S1][S2][S3][S5][S6][S7].

The policy head is left in FP16 because cuBLASLt's FP8 algorithm catalog on sm_120 requires the GEMM's N dimension (output_size) to be a multiple of 16, and for compact Xiang Qi, output_size = 105 is not a multiple of 16, causing cuBLASLt to reject the FP8 GEMM with NOT_SUPPORTED. [S1] [S2] [S15] [S17] [S19] [S20]

The checkpoint writer currently emits the V4 compact replay buffer schema, which includes the fields: input[input_dim], value, legal_indices[k_dim], target_values[k_dim], push_step, ply, opp_legal_indices[k_dim], opp_target_values[k_dim], opp_present, and state_bytes[state_bytes_dim] [S6]. Over the previous V3 layout, it appends the state_bytes field (for reanalyze state snapshot) as an extra target [S6][S10]. The markdown that describes this format as out of date is docs/replay_buffer_checkpoint.md, which still references V4 as current but does not reflect that pre-V4 schemas (V1–V3) are now refused and that the writer only emits V4 [S6][S8].

Values are stored and propagated in the leaf's `last_player_`'s perspective (the player who just moved into that state). [S1] During backup, a network value is negated if `last_player_ != cur_player_` for non-terminal leaves, and an ancestor's contribution is sign-flipped if its `LastPlayer()` differs from the leaf's `last_player_`. [S1]

Values are stored and propagated in the leaf's last_player_'s perspective, and a network value or ancestor's contribution is sign-flipped during backup when last_player_ differs from cur_player_ or when an ancestor's LastPlayer() differs from the leaf's last_player_. [S5]

Values are stored and propagated in the leaf's `last_player_`'s perspective (the player who just moved into that state). [S1] During backup, a network value is negated if `last_player_ != cur_player_` for non-terminal leaves, and an ancestor's contribution is sign-flipped if its `LastPlayer()` differs from the leaf's `last_player_`. [S1]

Values are stored and propagated in the leaf's `last_player_`'s perspective (the player who just moved into that state). [S1] During backup, a network value is negated if `last_player_ != cur_player_` for non-terminal leaves, and an ancestor's contribution is sign-flipped if its `LastPlayer()` differs from the leaf's `last_player_`. [S1]

The reanalyze worker uses the `SnapshotForReanalyze` API to obtain slot generations (push ids) for the rows it samples. [S2] It replaces only the `legal_indices` and `target_values` fields of the row, while carrying over unchanged the `input`, `value`, `push_step`, `ply`, `opp_*`, `state_bytes`, and `material_*` fields from the original row. [S3] [S4]

The reanalyze worker uses the `SnapshotForReanalyze` API to obtain slot generations (push ids) for the sampled rows. [S2]  
It replaces only the `legal_indices` and `target_values` fields of the row, while carrying over unchanged the `input`, `value`, `push_step`, `ply`, `opp_*`, `state_bytes`, and `material_*` fields from the original. [S3][S4]

The reanalyze worker uses the `SnapshotForReanalyze` API to obtain slot generations (push ids) for sampled rows [S2][S3]. It replaces the `legal_indices` and `target_values` fields while carrying over `value`, `push_step`, `ply`, `opp_*`, `state_bytes`, and `material_*` unchanged from the original row [S4][S5].

The policy head is left in higher precision (FP16) because its GEMM shape (M=B, N=output_size, K=d_model) does not satisfy the FP8 requirement that M, N, and K must all be multiples of 16, as output_size = 105 for compact Xiang Qi is not a multiple of 16. [S6] The body GEMMs dominate inference cost, so quantizing them to FP8 provides the primary speed-up while avoiding the complexity of FP8 for the head. [S6] Keeping the policy head in FP16 avoids re-casting before host softmax+sampling and allows the cuBLASLt epilogue to target CUDA_R_32F for that GEMM. [S5] The FP8 replica uses FP16 for the head and routes it through the existing FP16 GEMM. [S6] This design avoids quantizing the head to maintain numerical stability and compatibility with softmax and sampling operations. [S5] [S6]

The reanalyze worker uses the `SnapshotForReanalyze` API to obtain slot generations (push IDs) for the rows it samples. [S5]  
It replaces the `legal_indices` and `target_values` fields of the replay row with freshly computed MCTS results, while carrying over unchanged the `input`, `value`, `push_step`, `ply`, `opp_legal_indices`, `opp_target_values`, `opp_present`, `state_bytes`, `material_own`, and `material_opp` fields from the original row. [S11][S12]

The in-memory model cache is keyed by the sha256 of the checkpoint file, with eviction and reload triggered when the file's mtime changes [S1]. Concurrency is controlled via a per-model mutex (`search_mu`) that serializes `/infer` requests against the same model, while allowing interleaving across different models [S1]. The model-listing endpoint (`/models`) reports for each checkpoint: `path`, `sha256`, `mtime_unix`, `size_bytes`, and `schema_version` [S2].

The head weight `W_head` is kept in FP16 because `output_size` (105 for compact Xiang Qi) is not a multiple of 16, and cuBLASLt's sm_120 FP8 algorithm catalog rejects unaligned N with NOT_SUPPORTED, so the replica routes the head through the existing FP16 GEMM path. [S3] The body GEMMs dominate the inference cost, making it acceptable to leave the head in higher precision. [S3] The head GEMM shape (M=B, N=output_size, K=d_model) does not satisfy the alignment requirement for FP8 GEMMs, as output_size is rarely 16-aligned. [S6] cuBLASLt's FP8 algorithm catalog on sm_120 admits a body GEMM only when M, N, and K are all multiples of 16, which the head GEMM fails to meet. [S8] Therefore, the FP8 replica keeps `W_head` in FP16 and uses the FP16 GEMM path for the head. [S8] The head stays FP16 due to this hardware constraint, not because of computational dominance alone. [S1] [S3] [S6] [S8]

The in-memory model cache is keyed by the sha256 of the checkpoint file, with entries evicted and reloaded when the file's mtime changes [S1]. Concurrency is controlled via a per-model mutex that serializes concurrent requests against the same model, while allowing interleaving across different models [S1]. The model-listing endpoint reports for each checkpoint: path, sha256, mtime_unix, size_bytes, and schema_version [S2].

The training-time symmetry augmenter must avoid returning `target` unchanged, as that trains the network to be augmentation-invariant rather than equivariant. [S1] The scalar value target `target.z` must be preserved unchanged under symmetry, as board symmetries are score-preserving. [S1]

The documented bug is that returning `target` unchanged trains the network to be augmentation-invariant rather than equivariant, which is wrong [S1]. The scalar value target `target.z` must be preserved unchanged under symmetry [S1][S3][S4][S5].

The policy head is left in higher precision because cuBLASLt's FP8 algorithm catalog on sm_120 requires the N dimension (output_size) to be a multiple of 16, and for compact Xiang Qi, output_size = 105 is not a multiple of 16, causing the head GEMM to be rejected with NOT_SUPPORTED. [S1] [S2] [S7] [S8] The head weight `W_head` is therefore kept in FP16 and routed through the existing FP16 GEMM path to avoid this constraint. [S2] [S4] [S5] [S8] Since body GEMMs dominate inference cost, quantizing the head would not provide significant benefit and would complicate the implementation. [S2] [S8] The FP8 replica maintains FP16 for the head, embedding table, norms, and biases, while only quantizing body GEMM weights and activations. [S1] [S4] [S5] Softmax, DyT reductions, and head logits remain in FP32 for numerical stability. [S1] [S3] [S4] This hybrid precision layout ensures compatibility with cuBLASLt's FP8 support while maximizing performance gains from quantizing the computationally dominant body GEMMs. [S1] [S2] [S5] [S7] [S8]

The in-memory model cache is keyed by the sha256 of the checkpoint file, with eviction and reloading triggered when the file's mtime changes [S1]. Concurrency is controlled via a per-model mutex that serializes requests against the same model, while allowing interleaving across different models [S1]. The model-listing endpoint reports for each checkpoint: path, sha256, mtime_unix, size_bytes, and schema_version [S2].

The in-memory model cache is keyed by the SHA-256 of the checkpoint file, and if the file's mtime changes, the cache entry is evicted and reloaded on the next inference request. [S6] Concurrency is controlled by a per-cache-entry mutex that serializes concurrent requests against the same model, while requests against different models can interleave. [S6] The model-listing endpoint (`GET /models`) reports for each checkpoint: `path`, `sha256`, `mtime`, `size_bytes`, and `schema_version`. [S5]

The training-time symmetry augmenter must avoid returning the target unchanged, as that would train the network to be augmentation-invariant rather than equivariant. [S9] The scalar value target (z) must be preserved unchanged under symmetry. [S4][S9]

The board has only left/right mirror symmetry — the river divides the two sides asymmetrically and the palaces / pieces are tied to specific files. Rotations break the river / palaces; vertical flip would swap players (a perspective change, not a symmetry). [S1]

The documented bug is that returning `target` unchanged trains the network to be augmentation-invariant rather than equivariant, which is wrong [S1]. The scalar value target `target.z` must be preserved unchanged under symmetry [S1][S2][S3][S4][S5][S10].

The board has only left/right mirror symmetry — the river divides the two sides asymmetrically and the palaces / pieces are tied to specific files. Rotations break the river / palaces; vertical flip would swap players (a perspective change, not a symmetry). [S1]

Graphify's provenance defect was that nodes pointed to files mentioned inside transcripts instead of the transcript files containing the evidence, and the defect was in the source field of graph nodes. [S6]

The single board symmetry exploited for training is the left/right mirror across the central file (column 4). [S3]  
Rotations and vertical flips are excluded because they are not symmetries of the game: the river divides the two sides asymmetrically and the palaces are tied to specific files, so rotations break the river/palaces and a vertical flip would swap players (a perspective change, not a symmetry). [S3][S14]

Graphify's coding-session provenance was the blocking issue: in the final inception history graph, the nodes' `source_file` values pointed at files merely mentioned inside the transcripts (such as `.agents/skills/...`) rather than the transcript file that actually contained the evidence, so all three inception history queries missed their primary session file. [S3] [S2] [S1]

The local GraphRAG prototype achieved the strongest recall across both domains and projects with no LLM indexing tokens, reaching 1.000 recall@20 on all four project-domain pairs. [S1] The practical decision was not to replace `setting-up-rag` with graphify, as graphify needs provenance fixes and better code query seeding before it can be trusted for context-retrieval skills. [S1]

gpt-4.1-mini was chosen as the default OpenAI model for shirt thoughts. The first provider to return valid parsed text wins the race, and a fast error or empty provider response does not win. [S1][S2]

The default OpenAI model chosen was `gpt-4.1-mini` [S4]. The subtle rule that decides which provider's result wins the race is that the first successful parsed thought wins, not the first network response, so a fast error or empty provider response does not win [S4].

Enter submits the response on desktop, while Shift/Alt/Meta/Ctrl Enter and IME composition avoid submission; mobile/coarse-pointer blur submits the trimmed value. [S5]

Pressing Enter submits a full answer instead of adding a textarea newline; mobile/coarse-pointer blur can submit the same response. [S3]
